title: MSR 2026 research confirms AI-generated pull requests are rejected more often, but failure patterns are more predictable than human PR failures
summary: A peer-reviewed study presented at MSR 2026 (Mining Software Repositories, April 13, 2026) analyzed 1,779 rejected pull requests — 594 AI-generated and 1,185 human-authored — alongside 19,450 review comments and 28,875 review events from the AIDev dataset. The researchers developed a ten-category taxonomy of rejection reasons grounded in reviewer explanations and code-level evidence. The key finding is that AI-generated PRs are rejected at higher rates than human PRs, primarily due to incomplete implementation and testing inadequacy issues. However, a critical nuance emerged: human PR rejections tend to involve heterogeneous or context-specific reasons that vary widely, while AI PR failures demonstrate more consistent and predictable patterns with stronger predictive signals from description-level features. This predictability is significant because it means AI PR quality can potentially be improved through systematic feedback loops and automated pre-submission checks — a Circuit Breaker triage model using simple structural signals like patch size and files touched achieves strong discrimination for high-review-effort PRs. Separately, an analysis of 33,580 agentic PRs found that 2.66% contain at least one reverting commit, with substantial variation across agents (0.7% for OpenAI Codex to 7.6% for GitHub Copilot).
whyItMatters: The finding that AI PR failures are more predictable than human PR failures is strategically important: it means engineering teams can build targeted pre-submission filters and automated quality gates specifically calibrated to the consistent failure modes of AI-generated code, reducing review burden on human reviewers.

The dominance of incomplete implementation and testing inadequacy as AI-specific rejection reasons points to a fundamental limitation of current coding agents: they can generate plausible code but struggle with completeness verification and test coverage, suggesting that test-first development workflows may be especially important when using AI agents.

The 2.66% revert rate across 33,580 agentic PRs, with wide variation by agent (0.7% to 7.6%), gives engineering leaders concrete data for agent selection and risk assessment — not all AI coding agents produce equally reliable output.
recommendedActions: Implement pre-submission checks for AI-generated PRs based on the predictable failure patterns identified: verify implementation completeness against the stated objective and ensure adequate test coverage before human review begins.

Use the ten-category rejection taxonomy to train both AI agents and human reviewers: agents can be prompted to self-check against common failure categories before submitting, and reviewers can use the taxonomy as a structured checklist.

Track revert rates by AI agent tool to inform agent selection and configuration decisions — the 10x variation between agents (0.7% to 7.6%) represents a meaningful quality difference that should factor into tooling choices.
Consider implementing a Circuit Breaker triage system that uses structural signals (patch size, files touched) to automatically flag high-review-effort AI PRs for senior reviewer attention.
risksAndCaveats: The study analyzed open-source repositories from the AIDev dataset; rejection patterns in enterprise codebases with different review cultures, CI/CD pipelines, and coding standards may differ significantly.

The 1,779 rejected PR sample, while substantial, represents a fraction of total AI-generated PRs — the study focuses on rejections and does not provide overall rejection rates, making it difficult to calculate the absolute risk level.
Agent revert rates reflect early-2026 agent capabilities and may change rapidly as agents are updated — the 0.7% to 7.6% range is a snapshot, not a stable performance characteristic.
